dek: One of the richest counties in America, with top-ranked schools and the longest lives in Tennessee — and the widest gap in the pilot between what its households own and what its public realm offers.

sections.heading: A county that does the big things right
body: Williamson County is not a hard place to admire. The median household income here is $131,202 — first of Tennessee's 95 counties and about 167% of the U.S. figure — and the median home is worth $673,700, roughly 2.2 times the national number. That private wealth buys outcomes most places only wish for. People live to 81.3 years on average, the longest of any county in the state and about seven years above the Tennessee norm. Violent crime is among the lowest in the state, and by nearly every child-wellbeing measure this is one of the best places in America to raise a kid.

The schools deserve their reputation. Williamson County Schools is the #1 district in Tennessee — 67% math and 69% reading proficiency against roughly 34% and 37% statewide — and its students test about 1.8 grade levels above the national average. Nearly two in three adults, 62.5%, hold a bachelor's degree or higher, double the state rate. The systems work too: essentially 100% of residents have 100/20 Mbps broadband, and 74% can get gigabit. These are the pillars where the county leads, and it leads by a lot — Health & Safety and Education both score in the high 80s to low 90s.

sections.heading: Where the money didn't go
body: Now the receipts. For all that wealth, the shared, walkable, out-your-front-door part of life here lags badly. The Public Realm pillar scores around 37 and Stewardship around 34 — the two weakest in the county's whole profile — and the field survey shows why in plain terms.

This is a stoplight city. The ratio of roundabouts to signalized intersections is 0.03 — almost none — so movement means idling. On a recent Monday, Concord Road backed up a mile and a half at 6:30. Sidewalks measure 0.18 km per km of road, and the newest, most expensive subdivisions were built without them; the multi-use paths on some arterials are real but gap-ridden. And despite the immense wealth, Franklin's public playgrounds are dated — 1990s and 2000s equipment, mulch instead of rubberized surfacing (only one playground has it), peeling paint. Only about a third of residents, 33%, live within a ten-minute walk of a park, because the acreage that does exist — 11 acres per 1,000 people — is scattered across large-lot country and sprawling subdivisions where you have to drive to reach it.

sections.heading: Private domain over the commons
body: The pattern is not neglect so much as a choice about where wealth belongs. Williamson County put its money into the private domain — rural estates, gated amenities, subdivision pools and clubhouses — rather than the common wealth. You can see it in the built form. Downtown Franklin is genuinely excellent: intact 1800s stock, tasteful storefronts, the Harpeth Hotel. But it reads as a tourist downtown, not a living one, thin on walkable residential. Brentwood, the county's other wealth center, has no town center at all — it is pure sprawl.

The stewardship numbers tell the same story. Recent capital spending, modeled around $300 per resident a year, is driven almost entirely by school construction to keep pace with growth, not by streetscapes, parks, or sidewalks. Net public capital assets come to a modeled $4,500 per resident — modest set against $673,700 homes. There are bright spots worth naming: the library system logs about 1,780 visits per 1,000 residents, sixth of 185 Tennessee systems, and 132 National Register listings anchor a deep Civil War and antebellum heritage around Franklin. The commons here is not empty. It is just far smaller than the county could easily afford.

sections.heading: The contrast case
body: Williamson County earns a Civic Wealth Index of 62.5 out of 100 — a respectable score that hides the widest gap in the pilot between private and public wealth. The point is not that this is a bad place. It is a very good place that decided, over and over, to invest in what a family owns rather than in what a community shares. The schools are excellent, the lives are long, the incomes are the highest in the state. What lags is the realm you walk out into: the sidewalk that isn't there, the roundabout that isn't built, the playground that hasn't been touched since the 2000s. A county this rich could close that gap without breaking stride. So far, it mostly hasn't tried.
